title: Charmed Life — 2 (Fragment 2)
sourceText:
Miss Larkins seemed dumbfounded. “A man ? Not Bobby or Doddo—not a child’s voice, I mean?”

“No,” said Cat.

“How peculiar!” said Miss Larkins. “I never use a man. What did he say?”

Cat repeated what the voice had said. He thought he would never forget it if he lived to ninety.

It was some consolation to find that Miss Larkins was quite as puzzled by it as he was. “Well, I suppose it was a warning,” she said dubiously. She also seemed disappointed. “And nothing else? Nothing about your sister?”

“No, nothing,” said Cat.

“Oh well, can’t be helped,” Miss Larkins said discontentedly, and she let go of Cat in order to put her hair up again.

As soon as both her hands were safely occupied in pinning her bun, Cat ran. He shot out into the street, feeling very shaken.

And he was caught by two more people almost at once.

“Ah. Here is young Eric Chant now,” said Mr. Nostrum, advancing down the pavement. “You are acquainted with my brother, William, are you, Young Chant?”

Cat was once more caught by an arm. He tried to smile. It was not that he disliked Mr. Nostrum. It was just that Mr. Nostrum always talked in this jocular way and called him Young Chant every few words, which made it very difficult to talk to Mr. Nostrum in return. Mr. Nostrum was small and plumpish, with two wings of grizzled hair. He had a cast in his left eye too, which always stared out sideways. Cat found that added to the difficulty of talking to Mr. Nostrum. Was he looking and listening? Or was his mind elsewhere with that wandering eye?

“Yes—yes, I’ve met your brother,” Cat reminded Mr. Nostrum. Mr. William Nostrum came to visit his brother regularly. Cat saw him almost once a month. He was quite a well-to-do wizard, with a practice in Eastbourne. Mrs. Sharp claimed that Mr. Henry Nostrum sponged on his wealthier brother, both for money and for spells that worked. Whatever the truth of that, Cat found Mr. William Nostrum even harder to talk to than his brother. He was half as large again as Mr. Henry and always wore morning dress with a huge silver watch-chain across his tubby waistcoat. Otherwise, he was the image of Mr. Henry Nostrum, except that both his eyes were out of true. Cat always wondered how Mr. William saw anything. “How do you do, sir,” he said to him politely.

“Very well,” said Mr. William in a deep, gloomy voice, as if the opposite was true.

Mr. Henry Nostrum glanced up at him apologetically. “The fact is, Young Chant,” he explained, “we have met with a little setback. My brother is upset.” He lowered his voice, and his wandering eye wandered all around Cat’s right side. “It’s about those letters from—You Know Who. We can find out nothing. It seems Gwendolen knows nothing. Do you, Young Chant, perchance know why your esteemed and lamented father should be acquainted with—with, let us call him, the August Personage who signed them?”

“I haven’t the faintest idea, I’m afraid,” said Cat.

“Could he have been some relation?” suggested Mr. Henry Nostrum. “Chant is a Good Name.”

“I think it must be a bad name too,” Cat answered. “We haven’t any relations.”

words.definition: Extremely surprised or shocked.
words.example: Miss Larkins seemed dumbfounded.
words.exampleText: Miss Larkins seemed dumbfounded.

words.definition: Strange or unusual, especially in a way that is unsettling or hard to understand.
words.example: “How peculiar!” said Miss Larkins.
words.exampleText: “How peculiar!” said Miss Larkins.

words.definition: Comfort received by a person after a loss or disappointment.
words.example: It was some consolation to find that Miss Larkins was quite as puzzled by it as he was.
words.exampleText: It was some consolation to find that Miss Larkins was quite as puzzled by it as he was.

words.definition: In a doubtful or hesitant manner; with uncertainty.
words.example: “Well, I suppose it was a warning,” she said dubiously.
words.exampleText: “Well, I suppose it was a warning,” she said dubiously.

words.definition: Feeling sad or displeased because something was not as good as expected.
words.example: She also seemed disappointed.
words.exampleText: She also seemed disappointed.

words.definition: In a manner showing dissatisfaction or lack of contentment.
words.example: Miss Larkins said discontentedly.
words.exampleText: Miss Larkins said discontentedly.

words.definition: Fixing or fastening hair into place using pins.
words.example: She let go of Cat in order to put her hair up again, pinning her bun.
words.exampleText: She let go of Cat in order to put her hair up again, pinning her bun.

words.definition: Emotionally disturbed or upset.
words.example: Feeling very shaken, he ran into the street.
words.exampleText: Feeling very shaken, he ran into the street.

words.definition: Moving forward in a purposeful way.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum, advancing down the pavement.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum, advancing down the pavement.

words.definition: Having knowledge of or experience with someone or something.
words.example: You are acquainted with my brother, William, are you?
words.exampleText: You are acquainted with my brother, William, are you?

words.definition: Fond of joking; humorous or playful.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum always talked in this jocular way.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum always talked in this jocular way.

words.definition: Somewhat plump or slightly overweight.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum was small and plumpish.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum was small and plumpish.

words.definition: Having grey or partly grey hair, especially to denote age.
words.example: Two wings of grizzled hair.
words.exampleText: Two wings of grizzled hair.

words.definition: A condition in which the eyes do not properly align with each other; sometimes called a squint.
words.example: He had a cast in his left eye.
words.exampleText: He had a cast in his left eye.

words.definition: Moving about with no fixed purpose or direction; here used metaphorically for an unfocused gaze.
words.example: His wandering eye wandered all around Cat’s right side.
words.exampleText: His wandering eye wandered all around Cat’s right side.

words.definition: Somewhat plump or slightly overweight.
words.example: Mr. Nostrum was small and plumpish.
words.exampleText: Mr. Nostrum was small and plumpish.

words.definition: Having a lot of money; wealthy.
words.example: He was quite a well-to-do wizard.
words.exampleText: He was quite a well-to-do wizard.

translations.ru: пользоваться за чей-то счёт (жить за чужой счёт)

words.definition: To live at the expense of someone else without giving anything back; to leech off someone.
words.example: Mr. Henry Nostrum sponged on his wealthier brother.
words.exampleText: Mr. Henry Nostrum sponged on his wealthier brother.

words.definition: Short and fat; stout.
words.example: His tubby waistcoat.
words.exampleText: His tubby waistcoat.

words.definition: Dark or poorly lit, especially so as to appear depressing or frightening; also sad or pessimistic.
words.example: Mr. William in a deep, gloomy voice.
words.exampleText: Mr. William in a deep, gloomy voice.

words.definition: In a manner that shows regret or an apology.
words.example: Mr. Henry Nostrum glanced up at him apologetically.
words.exampleText: Mr. Henry Nostrum glanced up at him apologetically.

words.definition: A reversal or check in progress.
words.example: We have met with a little setback.
words.exampleText: We have met with a little setback.

words.definition: Held in great respect; valued highly.
words.example: Your esteemed and lamented father.
words.exampleText: Your esteemed and lamented father.

words.definition: Mourned for; regretted deeply, usually referring to someone who has died.
words.example: Your esteemed and lamented father.
words.exampleText: Your esteemed and lamented father.

words.definition: Perhaps; possibly.
words.example: Do you, Young Chant, perchance know why...?
words.exampleText: Do you, Young Chant, perchance know why...?

words.definition: A member of your family.
words.example: Could he have been some relation?
words.exampleText: Could he have been some relation?
